posts.name: Nicholas Rauch
posts.cooked:
I was hoping that somebody could walk me through the difference between PYCM’s standard en remilly bottling and the cuvée Nicholas et Mathis bottling.

I’ve had the standard en remilly quite a few times, like it quite a lot, and own a decent amount of it. Not going to beat a dead horse on such a highly-discussed wine. Is the Nicholas et Mathis a special plot that is better situated than the plots in the standard cuvée? I couldn’t find much information on this online, but it doesn’t seem that one is better than the other based on cellartracker scores alone, although I often disagree with cellartracker scores quite a bit

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
I remembered this from a previous discussion…

Trade tasting of Burgundy with some producers pouring including Pierre-Yves Colin WINE TALK
This should help: "Nicholas et Mathis is a special cuvée sourced from the very best part of Remilly, a plot perfectly positioned on the mid-slope of the vineyard, very near the Grand Cru site of Chevalier-Montrachet, which produces some of the most rare and expensive white wine in the world. En Remilly is the best site of St. Aubin. Seasoned Burgundy buyers have long sought out En Remilly for the ridiculous quality-price-ratio it delivers. And this helps to explain why Colin-Morey’s basic En …

link_counts.title: Trade tasting of Burgundy with some producers pouring including Pierre-Yves Colin

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
A similar write up from a recent Crush offer:

Colin-Morey makes two bottlings from En Remilly. Nicholas et Mathis is the rarer of the two bottlings. It’s situated near the Grand Cru site of Chevalier-Montrachet. Compared to Colin-Morey’s basic En Remilly, Nicholas et Mathis is deeper and more textural.

posts.name: Nicholas Rauch
posts.cooked: Thanks both! Have either of you tried it? I’m wondering how it actually compares in bottle to the standard offering. Sounds like purchased fruit rather than farmed, so could be that the difference in quality isn’t as simple as “better plot.”

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Yes I’ve had them both but never in a side by side. I typically seek out the N&M cuvée where possible, but have bought the regular when it was the only one offered at a good price. I think it generally just has a little bit more of what I want. But they are both good.

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
Bear in mind I haven’t had since 16/17 vintages but I don’t remember the cuvee being profoundly superior even at the modest markup it was back then ~$90 vs $60. As pycm pricing took off I stopped buying the NeM. Wish I would have saved a couple though, I’d imagine some benefit (or at least further differentiation) may emerge with more time in bottle?
Certainly a cool bottle to drink though.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked: I believe it’s a metayage arrangement. Can’t remember the owner but some big $$ dude. Similar to Dujac with its Chambertin.

posts.name: David Cooper
posts.cooked: I had the 08 and 09 at RN74 a few years ago. They were both incredible. We were told that one of Mina’s backers owned the plot.

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked:
NickRauch:

Thanks both! Have either of you tried it? I’m wondering how it actually compares in bottle to the standard offering. Sounds like purchased fruit rather than farmed, so could be that the difference in quality isn’t as simple as “better plot.”

The owner of the vineyard is Wilf Jaeger, he is a VC in the Bay Area. The vineyard is farmed by PYCM, it’s not purchased fruit, and as others have noted it is well sited and adjacent to Chevalier Montrachet.

I’ve been buying since 2007, and still have a couple of 07 and 08 left in the cellar. It’s a great wine that ages like a GC. I have done several tastings side by side, and for my palate the wine shows much more depth and complexity than the regular en Remilly bottling.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Larry_Link:

adjacent to Chevalier Montrachet

That would be a mild exaggeration. Chassagne-Montrachet En Remilly could be described as “adjacent” to Chevalier, but Saint-Aubin En Remilly is just pretty close… (the Chassagne En Remilly lies between this parcel and Chevalier). The aspect is also different.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:
Larry_Link:

adjacent to Chevalier Montrachet

That would be a mild exaggeration. Chassagne-Montrachet En Remilly could be described as “adjacent” to Chevalier, but Saint-Aubin En Remilly is just pretty close… (the Chassagne En Remilly lies between this parcel and Chevalier). The aspect is also different.

I also generally find the wines that are “adjacent” to a grand cru vineyard in many cases offer little resemblance to the actual wines from the grand cru.

posts.name: Nicholas Rauch
posts.cooked: While I was really hoping the ‘96 Clavelier Combe d’Orveau I just picked up would be a dead-ringer for a Musigny, this has generally been my experience on this issue as well
